quick_facts.label: Degree naming
quick_facts.value: The degree is offered under closely related titles. ICAR’s accredited-programme listings include “M.Sc. (Community Science) Food & Nutrition,” while Delhi University uses “M.Sc. Food and Nutrition.”

quick_facts.label: Relevant prior study
quick_facts.value: A B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science background is closely aligned with this pathway: ICAR lists institutions offering both B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science and M.Sc. (Community Science) Food & Nutrition. Exact admission eligibility remains university-specific.

quick_facts.label: Duration is not uniform
quick_facts.value: Programme length depends on the institution and the applicable NEP pathway. Delhi University approved a one-year coursework-and-research M.Sc. track for eligible four-year graduates in 2026, while the Institute of Home Economics describes its M.Sc. Food and Nutrition as a two-year postgraduate programme.

quick_facts.label: Core areas
quick_facts.value: Official programme material covers nutrition assessment and diet planning, clinical and public-health applications, food science and processing, food-service management, practical work, and research/project activity.

sections.heading: Eligibility after B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science
paragraphs: Eligibility is determined programme by programme rather than solely by the degree title. For example, Delhi University’s 2026 one-year M.Sc. Food and Nutrition route required a four-year Home Science honours degree with 28 credits of Food and Nutrition discipline-specific electives, plus category-specific minimum marks. Its published requirement is therefore an example of a specific institutional rule, not a nationwide rule for every Community Science graduate.

For a Community Science graduate, the practical check is whether the transcript contains the required Food and Nutrition coursework and credits. This is a natural academic progression at some agricultural universities: ICAR’s accredited-programme list records B.Sc. (Hons.) Community Science and M.Sc. (Community Science) Food & Nutrition at the same institutions. Applicants should obtain the target university’s current equivalence decision before assuming that their degree or individual papers qualify.
bullets: Keep semester-wise marksheets and the detailed syllabus/credit record available for verification.

Compare prior papers with the target programme’s requirements, especially nutrition, food science, biochemistry, physiology, microbiology, therapeutic/clinical nutrition, and public nutrition where specified.
Treat published percentage thresholds, category provisions, and accepted degree titles as admission-cycle-specific.

sections.heading: Duration and programme format
paragraphs: There is no single national duration for this canonical degree title. Delhi University’s Academic Council-approved NEP 2025 curriculum specifies a one-year, two-semester “Coursework + Research Track” for four-year B.Sc. Home Science graduates with the Food and Nutrition specialisation. In contrast, the Institute of Home Economics course page describes a two-year M.Sc. Food and Nutrition programme. Students should therefore verify both the master’s duration and the entry pathway—one-year versus two-year—before applying.

The one-year Delhi University coursework-and-research example carries 44 credits across two semesters and includes a six-credit dissertation, academic project, or entrepreneurship component in each semester. These details illustrate a current institutional design rather than a mandatory national template.
bullets: Confirm whether a university is admitting to a one-year or two-year master’s route for the relevant academic session.
Check whether the programme is a coursework-only route, coursework plus research, or requires a dissertation/project.
Do not infer duration from the M.Sc. title alone.

sections.heading: What students study
paragraphs: The curriculum normally integrates nutrition science with applied food systems and population health. Delhi University’s current one-year curriculum lists analytical techniques and instrumentation, nutritional epidemiology, food product development and quality evaluation, nutrition communication and health promotion, and dissertation/project/entrepreneurship work. Its elective pool includes medical nutrition therapy, institutional food-service management, public-health nutrition, maternal/infant/young-child/adolescent nutrition, food processing, exercise nutrition, and sport-specific nutrition.

Applied learning is material rather than incidental in the available official examples. The food-service management syllabus includes menu planning, quantity cooking, sanitation and hygiene work, and food-service surveys; the public-health nutrition material includes needs assessment, programme planning, monitoring, evaluation, nutrition surveillance, and field exposure. Course names, credits, electives, placements, and laboratory requirements can vary substantially across universities.
bullets: Nutrition assessment, epidemiology, therapeutic or clinical nutrition, and diet planning.
Public-health nutrition, communication, programme planning, monitoring, and evaluation.
Food product development, processing, quality evaluation, and food-service operations.
Laboratory/practical work plus a research, dissertation, project, or entrepreneurship component where prescribed.

sections.heading: Admission routes and application planning
paragraphs: Admission mechanisms vary. Delhi University’s two-year PG admissions for 2026–27 used CUET (PG) 2026 scores and its Common Seat Allocation System (CSAS), with applicants required to satisfy programme-specific eligibility. The Institute of Home Economics also states that applicants for its M.Sc. Food and Nutrition must meet programme-specific eligibility and take the relevant CUET paper. Other universities, especially agricultural universities, may use their own notified procedures or other entrance routes.

For a time-sensitive application, use the target university’s current admission bulletin as the controlling document. In Delhi University’s 2026 cycle, the university explicitly stated that allocation for two-year PG programmes would be based on CUET (PG) scores through CSAS; schedules, seats, test-paper requirements, and cut-offs should not be carried forward from a previous year.
bullets: Read the current programme-specific eligibility table before selecting an entrance test paper.
Apply through the university’s specified counselling/allocation portal after the required test, where applicable.
Verify seats, fee schedule, document-verification rules, and dates directly from the current institutional bulletin.

sections.heading: Further study and career directions
paragraphs: A research progression is available at institutions that offer doctoral study in the field. ICAR’s accredited-university listing includes examples where M.Sc. (Community Science) Food and Nutrition and Ph.D. Food and Nutrition are both offered. Doctoral admission requirements, entrance tests, research proposals, and fellowship conditions must be checked separately at each university.

The degree can support work in clinical/public-health nutrition, food science, food-service operations, quality assurance, research, and teaching-oriented pathways, depending on the electives, practical exposure, employer criteria, and any additional credentials required for a particular role. Delhi University’s curriculum identifies food scientist, quality-assurance manager/analyst, and food-service management as intended competency directions, while the Institute of Home Economics highlights public-health nutrition, dietetics, food science, academic, and research preparation. These are programme outcomes and directions, not guarantees of employment or authority to practise in a regulated role.
bullets: Ph.D. in Food and Nutrition or a related Community Science/Home Science discipline, subject to university-specific rules.
Research, academic, food-product-development, quality, food-service, and public-health-nutrition oriented roles.
Build role-specific competence through internships, supervised practical work, research projects, and any credentials required by the intended employer or professional setting.
